_purpose: Seasonal calendar for the TN East Great Valley cluster. Month-by-month agricultural, social, economic, and weather patterns across two eras (1770-1880 frontier/agricultural, 1880-1960 transitional/industrial). The Great Valley calendar was shaped by elevation and latitude — a shorter growing season than the Piedmont South, cold winters that killed tobacco in the field if the frost came early, and a corn-hog cycle that was the backbone of subsistence. The valley floor counties (Washington, Sullivan, Greene, Hawkins, Jefferson, Grainger) had a longer season than the mountain counties (Carter, Cocke). Tobacco replaced cotton as the cash crop — this was NOT cotton country. Court days at Jonesborough, camp meeting season, and the fall hog drove defined the social and economic calendar.
display_name: TN East Great Valley — Seasonal Calendar Profile
primary_economy: Subsistence corn-hog farming with tobacco as primary cash crop in valley counties (1770-1880); burley tobacco and diversified agriculture with emerging industrial wage economy (1880-1960); ginseng, chestnuts, and herbs as mountain cash supplements; livestock droving to Virginia markets

calendar_eras.description: The Great Valley calendar revolved around the corn-hog cycle and, increasingly, burley tobacco as a cash crop. The growing season ran approximately 160-180 days on the valley floor (late April to mid-October) but shortened to 130-150 days in the mountain counties. Corn was the subsistence crop — food for the family and feed for the hogs that provided meat, lard, and a marketable commodity. Tobacco was the cash crop in valley-floor counties, particularly after 1800 when burley varieties proved well-suited to the limestone soils. The fall hog drove — walking fattened hogs to Virginia markets along the Great Valley corridor — was the defining economic event of the autumn. Court days at Jonesborough and other county seats provided quarterly civic and commercial gatherings. Camp meetings in late summer were the social and spiritual climax of the year.

january.agricultural: Fields dormant under snow or frost. Hog killing continued if December weather had been too warm. Firewood cutting and fence rail splitting occupied the days. Tobacco stripping and grading in the stripping house — leaves sorted by color and quality, tied into hands, packed in hogsheads for spring transport to market. Mountain families in Carter and Cocke counties were effectively snowbound in bad years.
january.social_civic: Church services on Sunday where a minister was available — Presbyterian congregations had regular ministers; Baptist congregations might share a circuit rider who visited monthly. Visiting among neighbors limited by cold and mud. Quilting bees provided winter socializing and warmth (multiple bodies in one room).
january.economic: Tobacco stripping and grading was the primary economic activity. The cash from the previous year's tobacco sale was being spent on salt, iron, powder, and store goods at the crossroads store or Jonesborough. Credit extended against the next crop. Ginseng digging (dried root from fall harvest) sold to merchants for export to China via Virginia trading houses.
january.weather_conditions: Cold — average 30-38F. Hard freezes common. Snow cover in mountain counties (Carter, Cocke) could persist for weeks. Valley floor counties received less snow but endured bone-chilling dampness. The Great Valley funneled cold air from the northeast, creating wind chill on exposed ridges.
january.narrative_hook: January in {county} County was the stripping house and the fire — {ancestor_name} sorting tobacco leaves by lamplight while the Great Valley wind cut through the cabin logs, grading the year's cash crop by feel and color.

february.agricultural: Late-winter field preparation began on warmer days. Clearing new ground where timber had been girdled the previous year. Preparing plant beds for tobacco seedlings — seed sown in sheltered beds (often on a south-facing hillside) under brush covers to protect from late frost. Maple sap running in mountain counties.
february.social_civic: Limited social activity. Church services continued. County court in session at Jonesborough and other seats — quarterly court brought the scattered population together for legal business, news, and trading.
february.economic: Spring purchases at the store: seed, salt, plow points. Credit deepening. Maple sugar production in Carter and Cocke counties provided a small cash supplement.
february.weather_conditions: Late winter — 32-42F. Thaw-freeze cycles made roads impassable. Creek crossings dangerous with snowmelt runoff. The false springs of February — two warm days followed by a hard freeze — destroyed early plant beds if the farmer gambled.
february.narrative_hook: February was when {ancestor_name} gambled on the tobacco — setting plant beds on a south-facing slope behind brush covers, hoping the warm spell would hold and knowing a freeze could kill the seedlings and the year's cash with them.

march.agricultural: Plowing began in the valley floor counties as ground thawed. Corn ground prepared. Tobacco plant beds tended — seedlings growing under brush cover. Garden plots prepared near the cabin. Mountain counties still too cold for field work in early March.
march.social_civic: Spring court session at the county seat. Circuit riders resumed their rounds as travel became possible. Community gathered for communal tasks: log rollings, barn raisings.
march.economic: Store credit extended for spring supplies. Livestock assessed after winter — how many survived, what condition. Ginseng root and herbs sold to traveling merchants.
march.weather_conditions: Variable — 38-52F. Late snow possible. Spring rains began — heavy, sustained, turning the roads to mud. Dogwood and redbud blooming on the valley floor by late March.
march.narrative_hook: March in the Great Valley was mud season — {ancestor_name} breaking ground that had frozen and thawed all winter, watching the creek rise and calculating whether the tobacco beds had survived the last frost.

april.agricultural: Corn planting in earnest. Seeds dropped in prepared furrows. Tobacco seedlings growing in plant beds but not yet large enough to transplant. Garden planting: beans, potatoes, pumpkins, squash. Mountain counties just beginning to plant — Carter and Cocke ran two to three weeks behind the valley floor.
april.social_civic: Easter observances. Spring court day. Community labor exchanges for planting.
april.economic: Full credit extension at the store. The farmer committed to the season — seed in the ground, labor begun. No income until fall.
april.weather_conditions: Warming — 48-60F. Late frost remained a risk through mid-April on the valley floor and through early May in mountain counties. Rain critical for germination.
april.narrative_hook: When {ancestor_name} put corn in the ground in April {year}, {pronoun_subject} was betting the family's year on a valley that frosted late and rained enough — and every farmer on every creek from Jonesborough to Dandridge was making the same bet.

may.agricultural: Corn cultivation — hoeing between rows. Tobacco transplanting began in late May when seedlings were large enough (pencil-sized) to move from plant beds to field rows. This was the most labor-intensive week of the tobacco year — every plant set by hand, watered in, replanted where it died. Garden producing early greens, lettuce, radishes.
may.social_civic: Road work — county corvee labor required men to contribute days to road maintenance. Church services continued. The lengthening days expanded social life.
may.economic: No income. Store credit accumulating. Tobacco setting consumed all available labor — hired hands, neighbors in exchange, family.
may.weather_conditions: Warm — 56-68F. Thunderstorms frequent. A dry May could kill newly set tobacco; a wet May could rot the roots.
may.narrative_hook: May was tobacco setting — {ancestor_name} on hands and knees in {county} County, pushing each seedling into the dark valley soil, knowing that every plant that lived was a few more cents at market and every one that died was a hole in the year's income.

june.agricultural: Tobacco cultivation — hoeing, suckering (removing side shoots to force growth into the main leaves), worming (hand-picking tobacco hornworms). Corn growing tall. Wheat harvest in some valley floor areas. Hay cutting for winter feed.
june.social_civic: Church services. Beginning of the social season as days lengthened. Children out of school (where schools existed).
june.economic: No income. Credit deepening. Summer was the lean season — stores depleted from winter, new crop not yet producing.
june.weather_conditions: Hot — 64-76F. Humidity increasing. Thunderstorms. The valley trapped heat and humidity between the mountain ridges.
june.narrative_hook: June was worming and suckering — {ancestor_name} in the tobacco rows from dawn to noon, pulling hornworms off the leaves by hand and pinching suckers so the plant put everything into the leaves that would bring the money.

july.agricultural: Tobacco at peak growth. Continued cultivation and pest control. Corn tasseling. Garden at full production — beans, corn, squash, tomatoes. Ginseng scouting in mountain counties — locating patches for fall digging. Wild berries: blackberries, huckleberries gathered for preserves and fresh eating.
july.social_civic: Independence Day celebrations where communities observed them. Camp meeting season began in late July — the defining social and spiritual event. Week-long outdoor revival meetings drew families from across the county. Baptist and Presbyterian camp meetings were major social gatherings combining worship, courting, news, and commerce.
july.economic: No crop income. Lay-by period for corn allowed some families to do odd jobs for cash. Ginseng and herb gathering provided mountain families with future cash income (dried and sold in fall).
july.weather_conditions: Hottest month — 66-80F. Drought risk was the greatest danger to tobacco. The valley could be oppressively humid. Mountain counties were 5-10 degrees cooler.
july.narrative_hook: Camp meeting in July was when the Great Valley came together — {ancestor_name} with kin from three counties, the preacher's voice carrying over the creek, and the young people finding reasons to walk the grounds together after the sermon.

august.agricultural: Tobacco topping — cutting the flower head to force leaf growth. This was the final cultivation act before harvest. Corn maturing. Second cutting of hay. Early apples ripening. Late garden production.
august.social_civic: Camp meeting season at peak. Baptist and Methodist revivals — extended evening services. Courtship season for young people at the camp meetings and revivals. The August revival was the spiritual climax of the valley's year.
august.economic: Anticipation of harvest income. Assessment of tobacco crop quality — experienced farmers could estimate the year's return by examining the leaves.
august.weather_conditions: Hot and humid — 66-78F. Drought or hail could destroy the tobacco crop at this critical stage. Blue mold was a constant risk in wet years.
august.narrative_hook: August revival in {county} County was when {ancestor_name} heard something other than the hoe and the hornworm — the preacher's voice, the singing that carried across the bottom, and the altar call that offered something the tobacco crop never could.

september.agricultural: Tobacco cutting and curing — the harvest. Stalks cut and speared on sticks, hung in the curing barn. Burley tobacco was air-cured: barns with ventilated sides allowed air circulation while protecting from rain. The curing took four to eight weeks. Corn harvest began. Apple harvest. Sorghum cane cutting and processing for molasses.
september.social_civic: Fall court session at the county seat. Schools resumed (where they existed). Social life contracted as harvest demanded all available labor.
september.economic: The year's economic fate being determined in the curing barn. A good cure produced bright, elastic leaves. A bad cure — too wet, too fast, mold — destroyed the value.
september.weather_conditions: Moderating — 58-70F. The first cool nights were critical for curing — temperature drops helped the leaves cure evenly. An early frost could damage unharvested tobacco.
september.narrative_hook: September was the cutting — {ancestor_name} in the tobacco field at dawn, splitting stalks and spearing them on sticks for the barn, racing the frost that could ruin in one night what six months of work had grown.

october.agricultural: Tobacco curing continued in the barn. Corn harvest at peak. Hog fattening on mast (acorns, chestnuts) in preparation for December slaughter. Chestnut gathering in mountain counties — a significant cash crop before the blight. Apple butter making. Sorghum molasses boiling. Ginseng digging in mountain counties.
october.social_civic: Fall court day — the most important quarterly session. County fairs in larger communities. The fall hog drove began — fattened hogs walked to Virginia markets along the Great Valley road, a journey of one to two weeks. Court day at Jonesborough was the valley's largest civic gathering: legal business, horse trading, news exchange, political speeches, whiskey.
october.economic: Primary income season beginning. Tobacco being graded and prepared for market. Ginseng root sold to merchants. Chestnuts gathered and sold. The hog drove was a collective economic enterprise — neighbors combined herds and shared drovers.
october.weather_conditions: Cool and pleasant — 46-58F. First frost by mid-to-late October on the valley floor; earlier in mountain counties. The most comfortable month for outdoor labor.
october.narrative_hook: October court day at Jonesborough was when {ancestor_name} came down from the farm to settle accounts, hear the news, and watch the county's business conducted on the square — the one day the scattered valley gathered itself into something that felt like a community.

november.agricultural: Late corn harvest. Tobacco stripped and graded if curing is complete. Firewood cutting in earnest. Fence repair before winter. Turnip and root vegetable storage. Hog drove continuing or returning from Virginia markets.
november.social_civic: Thanksgiving observances. Hunting season — deer, turkey, bear (in mountain counties), small game. Hunting provided critical protein and, in mountain counties, cash from hides.
november.economic: Tobacco sales at market — hogsheads transported to Jonesborough or, after the railroad, shipped via rail to Virginia or Kentucky tobacco markets. Hog drove returns. Settling accounts with the merchant. The year's economic verdict delivered.
november.weather_conditions: Cold arriving — 36-48F. First hard freeze by mid-November. Snow possible in mountain counties. The Great Valley corridor funneled cold air.
november.narrative_hook: By November {year}, {ancestor_name} knew the answer — the tobacco graded and priced, the hog money counted, and the merchant's account settled or carried over. The valley darkened early, and the answer determined whether Christmas was relief or arithmetic.

december.agricultural: Hog killing — the central food event. The entire hog processed: hams smoked, sausage ground, lard rendered, head cheese made. Required cold weather (sustained below 40F) to prevent spoilage. Firewood the primary daily task. Fields dormant.
december.social_civic: Christmas — the paramount social event. Church services. Visiting among family and neighbors, sometimes over several days. Apple brandy and whiskey central to celebration. In the antebellum period, enslaved families on the modest East Tennessee holdings typically received a brief respite from labor. Shooting matches and other community competitions.
december.economic: Year-end accounting. Families that cleared their tobacco and store debt entered the new year free; families that did not carried the balance forward at interest. Christmas expenses modest by Lowcountry standards but significant relative to the subsistence economy.
december.weather_conditions: Cold — 28-38F. Hard freezes. Snow common, especially in Carter and Cocke counties. Mountain passes could close for days or weeks.
december.narrative_hook: Christmas in {county} County was when {ancestor_name} could stop — the hogs killed and salted, the tobacco money settled, and a few days of apple brandy, visiting, and rest before January put the world back on the cycle of plant, tend, cure, and sell.

calendar_eras.description: The railroad (1858) and later industrialization (1900-1930) transformed the Great Valley calendar without erasing it. Unlike the SC Piedmont, where the mill whistle replaced the sunrise entirely, East Tennessee experienced a hybrid calendar: valley-floor farm families continued the tobacco-corn cycle while industrial workers in Kingsport, Johnson City, Elizabethton, and Bristol shifted to factory time. The two calendars overlapped and intertwined — a Kingsport factory worker might tend a garden and keep hogs, and a Washington County tobacco farmer might send one son to the Eastman plant. TVA electrification (1930s-1940s) was the dividing line: before TVA, the farm calendar still governed most households; after TVA, electric light, refrigeration, and radio connected even mountain hollows to national time. Burley tobacco remained the primary cash crop through this entire period and beyond.

january.agricultural_industrial: Farm families: tobacco stripping, firewood, hog killing completion. Industrial workers in Kingsport, Elizabethton, Johnson City: full production resumed after brief holiday shutdown. Eastman Chemical and Mead Paper operated year-round; rayon plants in Elizabethton ran continuous shifts. The two calendars coexisted — a family might have the father at the Eastman plant and the mother managing the tobacco stripping at the home farm.
january.social_civic: Church services. Limited visiting. Company-sponsored social events in Kingsport (community building was part of the planned city's design). Mountain counties remained snow-isolated in bad years.
january.economic: Factory workers received weekly wages through the holiday period (unlike mill shutdowns in the Carolinas — Kingsport plants operated year-round). Farm families entered January spending last year's tobacco money and extending credit for the new year.
january.weather_conditions: Cold — 28-38F. Snow and ice. Industrial workers commuted on improved roads; farm families on mountain roads could be isolated for days.
january.narrative_hook: January {year} in {county} County split {ancestor_name}'s world in two — the brother at the Eastman plant drawing weekly wages through the cold, the family at the home farm stripping tobacco by lamplight and counting what was left of the fall money.

february.agricultural_industrial: Farm: tobacco plant beds prepared, maple sap tapped in mountain counties. Industry: full production. The Elizabethton rayon plants operated three shifts around the clock.
february.social_civic: Limited social activity. Church. Industrial workers' social life centered on company-provided facilities in Kingsport — recreation halls, organized sports.
february.economic: Factory wages steady. Farm credit deepening. The wage-farm hybrid economy was visible: families with one member drawing factory wages and others working the farm had more economic stability than pure farm families.
february.weather_conditions: Late winter — 30-42F. Thaw-freeze cycles. Roads between mountain farms and valley towns marginal.
february.narrative_hook: The factory wages that came every Friday in February were the difference between {ancestor_name}'s family and the families still dependent on the tobacco — cash in hand while the plant beds were still under brush covers and the crop was six months from money.

march.agricultural_industrial: Farm: plowing, corn ground preparation, tobacco bed tending. Industry: spring production. Zinc mining in Jefferson and Grainger counties operated year-round but increased spring hiring.
march.social_civic: Spring court session. Churches active. Community events resuming as weather improved.
march.economic: Farm credit extended. Factory wages steady. Zinc mine wages seasonal — spring hiring brought new workers into the mines.
march.weather_conditions: Variable — 38-52F. Mud season. Dogwood blooming on the valley floor.
march.narrative_hook: March in the Great Valley was when the two worlds showed their difference — {ancestor_name} at the factory gate in Kingsport at 6:00 AM on pavement, while a cousin ten miles up the hollow was breaking ground behind a mule on a road that would not see gravel until TVA came through.

april.agricultural_industrial: Farm: corn planting, tobacco beds growing. Industry: steady production. Garden planting universal — factory workers and farm families alike put in gardens.
april.social_civic: Easter observances. Industrial baseball leagues forming for the season. School sessions continuing (industrial towns had better-funded, more regular schools than rural areas).
april.economic: Farm: no income, credit running. Industry: steady wages. The economic gap between industrial and agricultural families widest in spring.
april.weather_conditions: Warming — 48-60F. Late frost risk. Spring rains.
april.narrative_hook: The garden behind the Kingsport company house and the garden behind the {county} County farmhouse were planted in the same April week with the same seeds — {ancestor_name} carrying the farming knowledge into the factory life because the valley taught everyone to grow food, wages or no wages.

may.agricultural_industrial: Farm: tobacco transplanting (the most labor-intensive week). Corn cultivation. Industry: full production. Some factory workers took leave to help family farms with tobacco setting — the kinship economy bridged the farm-factory divide.
may.social_civic: Road work in rural areas. Industrial town social life expanding with warmer weather. Decoration Day (Memorial Day) observances — deeply significant in the valley given Civil War division.
may.economic: Farm: maximum labor demand, no income. Industry: steady wages. Factory workers who helped family farms on weekends were contributing industrial income to the agricultural cycle.
may.weather_conditions: Warm — 56-68F. Thunderstorms. Tobacco setting required rain but not flood.
may.narrative_hook: Tobacco setting in May pulled {ancestor_name} back to the farm even from the factory — the family needed every hand to get the plants in the ground, and the factory calendar had to bend to the valley's oldest demand.

june.agricultural_industrial: Farm: tobacco cultivation, suckering, worming. Corn growing. Industry: full production through the summer. Zinc mines in full operation.
june.social_civic: Industrial town recreation: swimming, parks, organized company events in Kingsport. Farm communities: church, visiting as days lengthened.
june.economic: Farm: lean season. Industry: steady wages. The contrast was starkest in June — factory families buying groceries at the store while farm families ate from the garden and waited.
june.weather_conditions: Hot — 64-76F. Valley humidity. Mountain counties cooler.
june.narrative_hook: June in {county} County was the long wait — {ancestor_name} in the tobacco rows fighting hornworms while the cousin at the Eastman plant came home to a paycheck and a cold bottle from the icebox. Both were working; only one was getting paid.

july.agricultural_industrial: Farm: tobacco at peak growth, lay-by for corn. Industry: full production; some plants held brief maintenance shutdowns. Mountain families gathering herbs, berries, ginseng scouting.
july.social_civic: Independence Day — celebrated vigorously in both industrial towns and rural communities. Camp meeting and revival season beginning. The camp meeting tradition persisted even in industrial towns — workers attended evening services after factory shifts.
july.economic: Farm: lay-by — the one break. Mountain gathering economy: herbs, berries, ginseng preparation. Industry: steady wages with brief shutdown risk.
july.weather_conditions: Hottest month — 66-80F. Drought risk for tobacco. Mountain hollows 5-10 degrees cooler — a tangible advantage for mountain farming.
july.narrative_hook: July Fourth in {county} County was the one day the whole valley stopped — factory whistle silent, tobacco at lay-by, and {ancestor_name} at the creek bank with the family while the mountains shimmered in the heat and the firecrackers echoed off the ridges.

august.agricultural_industrial: Farm: tobacco topping, final cultivation. Industry: full production; fall orders building. Apple harvest beginning in mountain orchards.
august.social_civic: Revival season at peak — the defining cultural event persisted into the industrial era. Baptist and Methodist revivals drew workers from factory shifts. Courtship season. Company-sponsored events in Kingsport competed with traditional revivals.
august.economic: Farm: anticipation of harvest. Industry: steady. Apple sales from mountain orchards provided cash.
august.weather_conditions: Hot and humid — 66-78F. Blue mold risk for tobacco in wet years. Hail danger.
august.narrative_hook: Revival week in August was when {ancestor_name} heard something other than the factory whistle or the hoe — the preacher's voice under the arbor, the singing that carried down the valley, and the altar call that offered a moment outside the calendar of work.

september.agricultural_industrial: Farm: tobacco cutting and barn-curing. Corn harvest beginning. Sorghum molasses making. Industry: full production. The farm-factory split was sharpest at harvest — some factory workers took unpaid leave to help with tobacco cutting.
september.social_civic: Schools opened. Fall court session. Sorghum boiling was a community event — neighbors gathered to press cane and cook molasses, combining labor and socializing.
september.economic: Farm: the year's economic fate being determined in the curing barn. Industry: steady wages. Sorghum and apple sales supplemented farm income.
september.weather_conditions: Moderating — 58-70F. First cool nights critical for tobacco curing. Early frost danger.
september.narrative_hook: September was the cutting — {ancestor_name} in the tobacco field racing the frost, the curing barn filling with hung stalks, and the whole valley smelling of sorghum boiling and tobacco curing at the same time.

october.agricultural_industrial: Farm: tobacco stripping begins when curing is complete. Corn harvest. Chestnut gathering (until the blight, ca. 1930). Apple butter making. Ginseng digging in mountain counties. Industry: full production.
october.social_civic: Fall court day — the great civic gathering. County fairs. Church homecomings. After 1930, football replaced the hog drove as the fall spectacle — high school football in the Tri-Cities drew valley-wide attention.
october.economic: Primary farm income season. Tobacco graded and marketed (via rail after 1858, to Greeneville or Morristown tobacco warehouses). Ginseng, chestnuts (before blight), and root herbs sold to traveling buyers. The chestnut blight (arriving ca. 1925-1935) devastated mountain cash economy.
october.weather_conditions: Cool and pleasant — 46-58F. First frost. Peak fall color on the ridges.
october.narrative_hook: October in the Great Valley was when the year came to its verdict — {ancestor_name} at the tobacco warehouse watching the buyer examine the leaves, the grade and the price determining whether the family entered winter solvent or in debt.

november.agricultural_industrial: Farm: tobacco marketing continued. Firewood cutting. Hog fattening. Late corn harvest. Industry: production steady through Thanksgiving. Hunting season — critical for protein in farm households.
november.social_civic: Thanksgiving. Hunting: deer, turkey, bear (in mountain counties), squirrel, rabbit. Hunting was a male community event as well as an economic necessity — groups of neighbors hunted together for days.
november.economic: Farm: final tobacco sales, settling accounts with the merchant. Industry: steady wages. The economic divide between factory and farm families narrowed in November — both had income, both were preparing for winter.
november.weather_conditions: Cold arriving — 34-46F. First hard freeze by mid-November. Snow possible in mountain counties.
november.narrative_hook: November hunting in {county} County was not sport — it was protein. {ancestor_name} in the timber before dawn, the rifle an inheritance and the deer a necessity, because the tobacco money went to the merchant and the venison went to the family.

december.agricultural_industrial: Farm: hog killing — requiring sustained cold (below 40F). Firewood the daily task. Fields dormant. Industry: production continuing through most of December; brief holiday shutdown in most plants (shorter and often partially paid, unlike the Carolina mill shutdowns). Zinc mines operated through winter.
december.social_civic: Christmas — the paramount event. Church services, family visiting over several days. Apple brandy, whiskey, and by the industrial era, store-bought gifts supplementing handmade ones. Mountain families might be isolated by snow for the entire holiday.
december.economic: Year-end. Accounts settled. Factory workers with steady December income entered the new year more securely than farm families who depended entirely on the tobacco sale. The hybrid economy (factory wages plus farm production) was the most stable.
december.weather_conditions: Cold — 28-38F. Hard freezes. Snow common, heavy in mountain counties. Mountain passes and hollow roads could close for days.
december.narrative_hook: Christmas in {county} County was when {ancestor_name} could stop — the tobacco gone to market, the hogs hung in the smokehouse, and a few days of family, brandy, and firelight before January put the valley back to work.

narrative_hooks: The Great Valley calendar was written by tobacco — plant beds in February, setting in May, cutting in September, and the verdict at the warehouse in October. {ancestor_name} lived by that calendar, and every decision from credit to Christmas depended on what the leaves brought.

Camp meeting in late July was the one event the tobacco calendar could not touch — {ancestor_name} left the fields, met kin from across the county, and heard a voice that spoke to something the crop could not reach.

The factory whistle and the tobacco calendar ran side by side in the Great Valley — {ancestor_name}'s family straddled both, one member at the Kingsport plant drawing weekly wages while another stripped tobacco by lamplight at the home farm.

October court day at Jonesborough was when the valley gathered — {ancestor_name} among the farmers, merchants, lawyers, and horse traders on the square of the oldest town in Tennessee, conducting the business that could not wait for the next quarterly session.

Hog killing in December was the valley's oldest ritual — {ancestor_name} in the cold morning air, the hog scalded and scraped, every cut named and saved because the smokehouse was the family's winter bank.

Ginseng digging in October was mountain money — {ancestor_name} in the north-facing coves of {county} County, pulling roots that would travel from a hollw in Carter County to a merchant in Jonesborough to a ship bound for China.
source_references: Finger, John R. Tennessee Frontiers: Three Regions in Transition. Bloomington: Indiana University Press, 2001.
Eller, Ronald D. Miners, Millhands, and Mountaineers: Industrialization of the Appalachian South, 1880-1930. Knoxville: University of Tennessee Press, 1982.
Wolfe, Margaret Ripley. Kingsport, Tennessee: A Planned American City. Lexington: University Press of Kentucky, 1987.
Shackelford, Laurel, and Bill Weinberg, eds. Our Appalachia: An Oral History. Lexington: University Press of Kentucky, 1988.
Van Noppen, Ina Woestemeyer, and John J. Van Noppen. Western North Carolina since the Civil War. Boone, NC: Appalachian Consortium Press, 1973.
Hsiung, David C. Two Worlds in the Tennessee Mountains: Exploring the Origins of Appalachian Stereotypes. Lexington: University Press of Kentucky, 1997.
